displayTitle: 2025-11-10 - Justice Committee - Summary Offences (Demonstrations Near Residential Premises) Amendment Bill
topic: Summary Offences (Demonstrations Near Residential Premises) Amendment Bill
description: Monday 10 November 2025 | Justice Committee Summary Offences (Demonstrations Near Residential Premises) Amendment Bill Submitters The Law Association of New Zealand - Samira Taghavi, Convenor of the Public and Administrative Law Committee of TLANZ Lo Aleen
descriptionSummary: Summary Offences (Demonstrations Near Residential Premises) Amendment Bill. The Law Association of New Zealand. Samira Taghavi, Convenor of the Public and Administrative Law Committee of TLANZ

chunks.text: Is working. You know, we can use harassment law, intimidation law, the noise under the summary offenses act, under the harassment act, even even under the Crimes Act. We have law that could protect people if some of these utters actually go too far. But you need to bear in mind that most of the time the protest is for a legitimate reason.

And as a public figure, we always have to expect people protesting outside our private property. But as long as they're doing it properly and they're doing it, uh, in a way that is not causing trouble, then that should be accepted. That's a limit that we need to accept.

So basically the bill would limit three core rights under the Bill of Rights, the freedom of expression, freedom of peaceful assembly, and freedom of movement. Protecting privacy is important, but it's not more important than people's right to speak up and being heard. There's also really not clear evidence that protest outside homes are such a problem at the moment in New Zealand, that we need a new, that we need to create a new crime for it.

And introducing imprisonment for this kind of behavior is really, really extreme. So the other big problem is with the wording. The bill uses very vague terms like unreasonable disruption. This could almost mean anything depending on who's interpreting it.

It could be used to stop a small or peaceful protest outside for a cause. We really need to be careful. And also, another important point I wanted to make is that the Bill Evans says someone could be guilty if they out to have known that protest was disruptive. That is really not fair in criminal law.

You need to be very careful when it comes to wording. I'll give you one personal example. Uh, I am originally from Iran, as you all know. And during the serious human rights abusers in Iran, many of us in New Zealand have protested against the Revolutionary Guard. We have stood outside the Iranian embassy basically to demand justice and accountability.

I would've been proudly, proudly as a matter of principle to protest outside the ambassador's residence as well, which we had his address very peacefully and respectfully under this bill. This kind of action could be a criminal offense. I would hope that you all think I'm not really a criminal, but I'm trying to make a point here that even as someone who's educated, who's part of the community, we have a right to protest for a cause or for a, for, uh, as a matter of principle.

So look, protests are sometimes uncomfortable. They are meant to be uncomfortable. They are meant to challenge and disrupt. That's what drives the change. So if this law passes, people may be too afraid to speak out. So you're really going to silence minority voices and I think it's a huge blow to the Democratic accountability.

So if you are still concerned about these targeted protests, there are better options. Use civil injunctions, use trespass notices, give police a clear guidance on how to use their existing power. Let local council deal with noise and obstruction. As I sit again and I repeat that, I haven't yet seen clear evidence as why this is such a big problem that we need new law.

However, if the committee still decides to proceed, please make it safer and please make it fairer. Remove imprisonment, keep it only findable. Require actual intent, not just because someone out to have known and clearly define unreasonable disruption so people actually know what is allowed and what is not allowed.

And also add a clause confirming the bill must be interpreted consistently with the Bill of Rights. So, long story short, I believe the bill is too broad, too vague, and too dangerous. So please, when you're considering our submissions, be very careful in terms of what's going to come next.

Uh, my name is Lo I'm an Armenian American and I've been living in Al Tera for almost two years now. Um, and for the last two years I've been watching in horror as my home country has been descending into authoritarianism. I have friends in the US who have been threatened, assaulted by police and expelled from university simply for participating in nonviolent protests, protests against the illegal occupation of Palestine and against the illegal abducting and torture of US citizens without trial.

But what does it mean to say that those actions are illegal when US lawmakers can snap their fingers and say that it is actually my peace-loving friends who are the criminals? The US claims that it values law and order, but we can see clear as day that the United States cracked down on protest, has made a mockery of the law and has only caused more chaos across the entire country.

This is the example that New Zealand is trying to follow with this bill with its vague, unclear wording. The police could decide one day that a couple of people gathered on Cuba Street are a disruption to the people living nearby and arrest everyone for exercising their right to free expression. Is this the kind of community we want to live in where people are afraid to speak out because anything they do or say could be grounds for arrest depending on the whims of the police?

We've seen the strategy backfire dramatically and shamefully in the US and UK and all the while I've been saying, thank God that New Zealand isn't doing this, isn't treating people like that. I thought that this country valued its diverse opinions and valued its history of anti-nuclear and anti-apartheid protests.

And now it feels like a punch to the gut to see New Zealand follow the script that America has written when we all know that it will only lead to repression and authoritarianism. I hope that New Zealand will change course and listen to the people. Unfortunately, parliament didn't listen to the thousands of people who oppose the Fast Track bill or to the 98% of submissions against the regulatory standards bill.

But over the last few weeks, you've all been hearing so many reasons why this bill is a threat to people's freedoms and wellbeing. And I hope that this will not be another example of New Zealand parliament refusing to listen to the people that they are meant to represent. I think having listened to a lot of the previous submissions, I think a lot of members of parliament are probably scared that they are going to be targeted by protestors.

You might be worried that some people are going to chant outside your house and interrupt your sleep, but try for a moment to empathize with the normal everyday people who you are meant to represent. If it was your family in Gaza being bombed, wouldn't you want people to protest to stop it? If it was your neighborhood getting flooded because of climate change, wouldn't you want people to protest to stop it?

And if it was you and the people you love being portrayed as criminals simply for having the bravery to stand up for what is right, wouldn't you want to stop this bill from passing? But let me tell you, even despite all the chaos and repression and governmental overreach in the United States last week, I watched Zoran Momani get elected as mayor of New York City and he said, quote, we chose hope together, hope over tyranny, hope over big money and small ideas, hope over despair.

He said that politics should be something that we all do together, not something that is done to us. So I believe that in the US and in New Zealand, it is more critical than ever to up uplift people's voices rather than criminalizing them. As this bill is trying to do. The people of New Zealand deserve the right to speak up when something is wrong.

And they are speaking now saying that this bill is wrong and will set us down a concerning path towards even more restrictions and regressions. I hope that you members of Parliament will listen and protect the right to protest. I hope that you do not pass this bill and I hope that you help set us on a path to peace and justice instead of authoritarianism. Kiara, Hey, thank you very much for that.
